explanation_md: The constructor `Box(int side)` runs with argument `7` when `Box b{ 7 }` is created, storing `7` in `m_side`. `getSide()` returns the stored value, which `std::cout` prints as `7`.

prompt_md: Which of the following is a correct constructor declaration for a class named `Sensor`?
explanation_md: A constructor is declared with only the class name and a parameter list — no return type at all, not even `void`. Options a, b, and d all include a return type, which is a compile error for constructors.

`Label a` calls the zero-argument constructor, setting `m_text` to `"default"`. `Label b{ "hello" }` calls the one-argument constructor, setting `m_text` to `"hello"`. Each `getText()` returns the respective string.

prompt_md: When exactly does a constructor run?

options.a: The first time any member function is called on the object.
options.b: Automatically, once, at the moment the object is created.
options.d: At the end of the block in which the object is declared.

explanation_md: A constructor runs automatically, exactly once, at the moment the object comes into existence — before any other code can touch the object. You never call it explicitly by name after the object exists. This is what guarantees that the object is in a valid state from the very start.

options.a: `m_x` and `m_y` are private, so the constructor cannot assign to them.
options.b: The constructor only initializes `m_x`; `m_y` is never set, so reading it is undefined behavior.
options.c: The constructor must be declared `const` to set member values.
options.d: `Point` needs a destructor to clean up `m_x` and `m_y`.

explanation_md: `m_y` has no in-class initializer and the constructor body only assigns `m_x`. Reading an uninitialized variable is undefined behavior — the program may print anything. The fix is to add `m_y = 0;` (or a suitable default) to the constructor, or add an in-class initializer `int m_y{ 0 };`.
